title: Day 15 Interview Prep
description: Practice Context vs props, when NOT to use Context, global state decisions, and this/call/apply/bind from today's roadmap.

questions.question: Context vs Props — when do you use each?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Practical state-sharing judgment, not Context for everything.
keyPoints: Props for direct parent-child and shallow trees
Context for widely consumed global data like theme, locale, auth
Prop drilling is not always bad for 2–3 levels
Composition can replace some Context needs
questions.sampleAnswer: I use props when data flows one or two levels and the parent owns the state. I use Context when many distant components need the same data — theme, auth, locale. I do not reach for Context just to avoid passing two props. I also consider composition: passing children as props sometimes removes drilling without Context.
followUps: Can composition replace Context for theme?

questions.question: When should you NOT use React Context?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Awareness of Context limitations and overuse.
keyPoints: Frequently changing data causes wide re-renders
Not a full state management replacement
Server state belongs in data-fetching libraries
Local UI state stays in components
questions.sampleAnswer: I avoid Context for high-frequency updates like keystroke search or mouse position — every consumer re-renders. I avoid it when props or composition suffice. I do not use Context alone for complex server-cache logic — that is RTK Query or React Query territory. Context fits stable, globally read preferences like theme.
followUps: How split context to reduce re-renders?

questions.question: What is global state and what belongs in it?
keyPoints: State needed across many unrelated components
Not every useState should be global
questions.sampleAnswer: Global state is data many parts of the app read or write without a common parent — theme mode, authenticated user, feature flags. Local state stays local: form inputs, modal open, hover. I promote to global only when prop drilling hurts maintainability or multiple features need the same truth.
followUps: Where store global state without Redux?

questions.question: Explain call, apply, and bind with examples.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Classic JS fundamentals still asked at Indian product companies.
keyPoints: Arrow functions ignore bind/call/apply for this
questions.sampleAnswer: call and apply invoke a function with an explicit this. greet.call(user, 'Hi') passes args individually; greet.apply(user, ['Hi']) passes an array. bind returns a new function with this locked: const bound = greet.bind(user) — useful for passing methods as callbacks without losing this. Arrow functions inherit lexical this so bind does not change their this.
followUps: What is this in strict mode?
Why do React class components use bind?

questions.question: How do you type a React Context in TypeScript?
keyPoints: Custom hook throws if context is null
questions.sampleAnswer: I define ThemeContextValue with theme: ThemePreference and setTheme: (t: ThemePreference) => void. I create context with createContext<ThemeContextValue | null>(null). My useTheme hook reads context, throws if null — meaning used outside provider — and returns the typed value. This gives compile-time safety and runtime guardrails.

questions.question: How would you build a theme system with light, dark, and system modes?
questions.sampleAnswer: I store user preference as light | dark | system. Resolved theme is light or dark — system reads prefers-color-scheme. ThemeProvider holds preference state, resolves theme, applies data-theme on documentElement, persists to localStorage. useTheme exposes both. I memoize context value and subscribe to media query changes for system mode.
followUps: CSS variables vs Tailwind dark mode?

questions.question: Your app re-renders 50 components when theme toggles. How do you investigate and optimize?
keyPoints: Theme change should re-render themed UI — that is expected
questions.sampleAnswer: I use React DevTools profiler and highlight updates to see which components re-render. If everything re-renders on unrelated parent state, the provider likely passes a new object each render — fix with useMemo. I split ThemeStateContext and ThemeDispatchContext so consumers that only call setTheme do not re-render on theme change. Some re-render on theme change is correct — CSS variables need to apply.

questions.question: Theme resets to light on every refresh despite localStorage. Walk through your debug process.
keyPoints: Check localStorage key and value in DevTools Application tab
questions.sampleAnswer: I check Application → Local Storage for the key and value. If write works but read fails, I likely read in useEffect — first render uses default before effect runs. Fix: useState(() => localStorage.getItem(KEY) ?? 'system'). If key mismatch between write and read, align constants. If flash then correct, add synchronous read or inline head script.

questions.question: A junior puts cart state, theme, and API cache all in one Context. How do you refactor?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Separation of concerns and state architecture mentoring.
keyPoints: Single context with unrelated data causes massive re-renders
Split by update frequency and domain
Preview Redux for cart without implementing today
questions.sampleAnswer: I explain that one mega-context means any cart quantity change re-renders theme consumers. I split: ThemeContext for stable preference, cart moves to useReducer+Context or Redux Toolkit tomorrow, API cache to RTK Query next week. I draw boundaries by update frequency and consumer count. I pair on extracting ThemeProvider first as the smallest win.
followUps: How many contexts is too many?
redFlags: One context for everything is simpler

questions.question: Compare three approaches to global theme: CSS class on html, Context, and Redux. Which do you pick and why?
keyPoints: Context is the React state layer
Redux adds DevTools and middleware for large apps
Theme is low-frequency — Context usually sufficient
questions.sampleAnswer: CSS class or data-theme on html is how the browser applies styles — every approach needs that DOM hook. Context provides React state and a useTheme hook for components. Redux adds persistence middleware, time-travel, and consistency when theme interacts with many slices — overkill for theme alone. I pick Context + localStorage for most apps; Redux when theme is one piece of a unified global store already using RTK.
